docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Basic 2-dimensional vector, represented as (x,y). Values can be numeric, or NaN or infinite.

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.title: Class Vector2
docs.text: import { Vector2 } from 'scenerystack/dot';

docs.title: new Vector2( x : number, y : number )
getMagnitude() : number
docs.text: The magnitude (Euclidean/L2 Norm) of this vector, i.e. \(\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\).

docs.title: getMagnitudeSquared() : number
docs.text: The squared magnitude (square of the Euclidean/L2 Norm) of this vector, i.e. \(x^2+y^2\).

docs.title: distance( point : Vector2 ) : number
docs.text: The Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point.

docs.title: distanceXY( x : number, y : number ) : number
docs.text: The Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point (x,y).

docs.title: distanceSquared( point : Vector2 ) : number
docs.text: The squared Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point.

docs.title: distanceSquaredXY( x : number, y : number ) : number
docs.text: The squared Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point with coordinates (x,y).

docs.title: dot( v : Vector2 ) : number
docs.text: The dot-product (Euclidean inner product) between this vector and another vector v.

docs.title: dotXY( x : number, y : number ) : number
docs.text: The dot-product (Euclidean inner product) between this vector and another vector (x,y).

docs.title: getAngle() : number
docs.text: The angle \(\theta\) of this vector, such that this vector is equal to $$ u = \begin{bmatrix} r\cos\theta \ r\sin\theta \end{bmatrix} $$ for the magnitude \(r \ge 0\) of the vector, with \(\theta\in(-\pi,\pi]\)

docs.title: angleBetween( v : Vector2 ) : number
docs.text:
The angle between this vector and another vector, in the range \(\theta\in[0, \pi]\).

Equal to \(\theta = \cos^{-1}( \hat{u} \cdot \hat{v} )\) where \(\hat{u}\) is this vector (normalized) and \(\hat{v}\) is the input vector (normalized).

docs.title: equals( other : Vector2 ) : boolean
docs.text:
Exact equality comparison between this vector and another vector.

@returns - Whether the two vectors have equal components

docs.title: equalsEpsilon( other : Vector2, epsilon : number ) : boolean
docs.text:
Approximate equality comparison between this vector and another vector.

@returns - Whether difference between the two vectors has no component with an absolute value greater than epsilon.

docs.title: isFinite() : boolean
docs.text: Returns false if either component is NaN, infinity, or -infinity. Otherwise returns true.

docs.title: copy( vector? : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Creates a copy of this vector, or if a vector is passed in, set that vector's values to ours.

This is the immutable form of the function set(), if a vector is provided. This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

@param [vector] - If not provided, creates a new Vector2 with filled in values. Otherwise, fills in the values of the provided vector so that it equals this vector.

docs.title: crossScalar( v : Vector2 ) : number
docs.text: The scalar value of the z-component of the equivalent 3-dimensional cross product: $$ f( u, v ) = \left( \begin{bmatrix} u_x \ u_y \ 0 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} v_x \ v_y \ 0 \end{bmatrix} \right)_z = u_x v_y - u_y v_x $$

docs.title: normalized() : Vector2
docs.text:
Normalized (re-scaled) copy of this vector such that its magnitude is 1. If its initial magnitude is zero, an error is thrown.

This is the immutable form of the function normalize(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: roundedSymmetric() : Vector2
docs.text:
Returns a copy of this vector with each component rounded by Utils.roundSymmetric.

This is the immutable form of the function roundSymmetric(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: withMagnitude( magnitude : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Re-scaled copy of this vector such that it has the desired magnitude. If its initial magnitude is zero, an error is thrown. If the passed-in magnitude is negative, the direction of the resulting vector will be reversed.

This is the immutable form of the function setMagnitude(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: timesScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Copy of this vector, scaled by the desired scalar value.

This is the immutable form of the function multiplyScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: times( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Same as timesScalar.

This is the immutable form of the function multiply(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: componentTimes( v : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Copy of this vector, multiplied component-wise by the passed-in vector v.

This is the immutable form of the function componentMultiply(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: plus( v : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Addition of this vector and another vector, returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function add(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: plusXY( x : number, y : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Addition of this vector and another vector (x,y), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function addXY(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: plusScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Addition of this vector with a scalar (adds the scalar to every component), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function addScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: minus( v : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Subtraction of this vector by another vector v, returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function subtract(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: minusXY( x : number, y : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Subtraction of this vector by another vector (x,y), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function subtractXY(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: minusScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Subtraction of this vector by a scalar (subtracts the scalar from every component), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function subtractScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: dividedScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Division of this vector by a scalar (divides every component by the scalar), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function divideScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: negated() : Vector2
docs.text:
Negated copy of this vector (multiplies every component by -1).

This is the immutable form of the function negate(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: getPerpendicular() : Vector2
docs.text: Rotated by -pi/2 (perpendicular to this vector), returned as a copy.

docs.title: rotated( angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Rotated by an arbitrary angle, in radians. Returned as a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function rotate(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

@param angle - In radians

docs.title: rotateAboutXY( x : number, y : number, angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Mutable method that rotates this vector about an x,y point.

@param x - origin of rotation in x @param y - origin of rotation in y @param angle - radians to rotate @returns this for chaining

docs.title: rotateAboutPoint( point : Vector2, angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text: Same as rotateAboutXY but with a point argument.

docs.title: rotatedAboutXY( x : number, y : number, angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Immutable method that returns a new Vector2 that is rotated about the given point.

@param x - origin for rotation in x @param y - origin for rotation in y @param angle - radians to rotate

docs.title: rotatedAboutPoint( point : Vector2, angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text: Immutable method that returns a new Vector2 rotated about the given point.

docs.title: blend( vector : Vector2, ratio : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
A linear interpolation between this vector (ratio=0) and another vector (ratio=1).

@param vector @param ratio - Not necessarily constrained in [0, 1]

docs.title: average( vector : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text: The average (midpoint) between this vector and another vector.

docs.title: toString() : string
docs.text: Debugging string for the vector.

docs.title: toVector3() : Vector3
docs.text: Converts this to a 3-dimensional vector, with the z-component equal to 0.

docs.title: setXY( x : number, y : number ) : Vector2
docs.text: Sets all of the components of this vector, returning this.

docs.title: setX( x : number ) : Vector2
docs.text: Sets the x-component of this vector, returning this.

docs.title: setY( y : number ) : Vector2
docs.text: Sets the y-component of this vector, returning this.

docs.title: set( v : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Sets this vector to be a copy of another vector.

This is the mutable form of the function copy(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: setMagnitude( magnitude : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Sets the magnitude of this vector. If the passed-in magnitude is negative, this flips the vector and sets its magnitude to abs( magnitude ).

This is the mutable form of the function withMagnitude(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: add( v : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Adds another vector to this vector, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function plus(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: addXY( x : number, y : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Adds another vector (x,y) to this vector, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function plusXY(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: addScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Adds a scalar to this vector (added to every component), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function plusScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: subtract( v : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Subtracts this vector by another vector, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function minus(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: subtractXY( x : number, y : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Subtracts this vector by another vector (x,y), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function minusXY(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: subtractScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Subtracts this vector by a scalar (subtracts each component by the scalar), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function minusScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: multiplyScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Multiplies this vector by a scalar (multiplies each component by the scalar), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function timesScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: multiply( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Multiplies this vector by a scalar (multiplies each component by the scalar), changing this vector. Same as multiplyScalar.

This is the mutable form of the function times(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: componentMultiply( v : Vector2 ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Multiplies this vector by another vector component-wise, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function componentTimes(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: divideScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Divides this vector by a scalar (divides each component by the scalar), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function dividedScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: negate() : Vector2
docs.text:
Negates this vector (multiplies each component by -1), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function negated(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: normalize() : Vector2
docs.text:
Normalizes this vector (rescales to where the magnitude is 1), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function normalized(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: roundSymmetric() : Vector2
docs.text:
Rounds each component of this vector with Utils.roundSymmetric.

This is the mutable form of the function roundedSymmetric(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning the vector itself.

docs.title: rotate( angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Rotates this vector by the angle (in radians), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function rotated(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

@param angle - In radians

docs.title: setPolar( magnitude : number, angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Sets this vector's value to be the x,y values matching the given magnitude and angle (in radians), changing this vector, and returning itself.

@param magnitude @param angle - In radians

docs.title: toStateObject() : Vector2StateObject
docs.text:
Returns a duck-typed object meant for use with tandem/phet-io serialization. Although this is redundant with stateSchema, it is a nice feature of such a heavily-used type to be able to call toStateObject directly on the type.

@returns - see stateSchema for schema

docs.text: Returns a Vector2 with the specified magnitude \(r\) and angle \(\theta\) (in radians), with the formula: $$ f( r, \theta ) = \begin{bmatrix} r\cos\theta \ r\sin\theta \end{bmatrix} $$

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Basic 3-dimensional vector, represented as (x,y,z).

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.title: Class Vector3
docs.text: import { Vector3 } from 'scenerystack/dot';

docs.title: new Vector3( x : number, y : number, z : number )
getMagnitude() : number
docs.text: The magnitude (Euclidean/L2 Norm) of this vector, i.e. \(\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}\).

docs.title: getMagnitudeSquared() : number
docs.text: T squared magnitude (square of the Euclidean/L2 Norm) of this vector, i.e. \(x^2+y^2+z^2\).

docs.title: distance( point : Vector3 ) : number
docs.text: The Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point.

docs.title: distanceXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : number
docs.text: The Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point (x,y,z).

docs.title: distanceSquared( point : Vector3 ) : number
docs.text: The squared Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point.

docs.title: distanceSquaredXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : number
docs.text: The squared Euclidean distance between this vector (treated as a point) and another point (x,y,z).

docs.title: dot( v : Vector3 ) : number
docs.text: The dot-product (Euclidean inner product) between this vector and another vector v.

docs.title: dotXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : number
docs.text: The dot-product (Euclidean inner product) between this vector and another vector (x,y,z).

docs.title: angleBetween( v : Vector3 ) : number
docs.text:
The angle between this vector and another vector, in the range \(\theta\in[0, \pi]\).

Equal to \(\theta = \cos^{-1}( \hat{u} \cdot \hat{v} )\) where \(\hat{u}\) is this vector (normalized) and \(\hat{v}\) is the input vector (normalized).

docs.title: equals( other : Vector3 ) : boolean
docs.text:
Exact equality comparison between this vector and another vector.

@returns - Whether the two vectors have equal components

docs.title: equalsEpsilon( other : Vector3, epsilon : number ) : boolean
docs.text:
Approximate equality comparison between this vector and another vector.

@returns - Whether difference between the two vectors has no component with an absolute value greater than epsilon.

docs.title: isFinite() : boolean
docs.text: Returns false if any component is NaN, infinity, or -infinity. Otherwise returns true.

docs.title: copy( vector? : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Creates a copy of this vector, or if a vector is passed in, set that vector's values to ours.

This is the immutable form of the function set(), if a vector is provided. This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

@param [vector] - If not provided, creates a new Vector3 with filled in values. Otherwise, fills in the values of the provided vector so that it equals this vector.

docs.title: cross( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text: The Euclidean 3-dimensional cross-product of this vector by the passed-in vector.

docs.title: normalized() : Vector3
docs.text:
Normalized (re-scaled) copy of this vector such that its magnitude is 1. If its initial magnitude is zero, an error is thrown.

This is the immutable form of the function normalize(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: roundedSymmetric() : Vector3
docs.text: This is the immutable form of the function roundSymmetric(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: withMagnitude( magnitude : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Re-scaled copy of this vector such that it has the desired magnitude. If its initial magnitude is zero, an error is thrown. If the passed-in magnitude is negative, the direction of the resulting vector will be reversed.

This is the immutable form of the function setMagnitude(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: timesScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Copy of this vector, scaled by the desired scalar value.

This is the immutable form of the function multiplyScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: times( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Same as timesScalar.

This is the immutable form of the function multiply(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: componentTimes( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Copy of this vector, multiplied component-wise by the passed-in vector v.

This is the immutable form of the function componentMultiply(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: plus( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Addition of this vector and another vector, returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function add(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: plusXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Addition of this vector and another vector (x,y,z), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function addXYZ(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: plusScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Addition of this vector with a scalar (adds the scalar to every component), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function addScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: minus( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Subtraction of this vector by another vector v, returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function subtract(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: minusXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Subtraction of this vector by another vector (x,y,z), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function subtractXYZ(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: minusScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Subtraction of this vector by a scalar (subtracts the scalar from every component), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function subtractScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: dividedScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Division of this vector by a scalar (divides every component by the scalar), returning a copy.

This is the immutable form of the function divideScalar(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: negated() : Vector3
docs.text:
Negated copy of this vector (multiplies every component by -1).

This is the immutable form of the function negate(). This will return a new vector, and will not modify this vector.

docs.title: blend( vector : Vector3, ratio : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
A linear interpolation between this vector (ratio=0) and another vector (ratio=1).

@param vector @param ratio - Not necessarily constrained in [0, 1]

docs.title: average( vector : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text: The average (midpoint) between this vector and another vector.

docs.title: toString() : string
docs.text: Debugging string for the vector.

docs.title: toVector2() : Vector2
docs.text: Converts this to a 2-dimensional vector, discarding the z-component.

docs.title: toVector4() : Vector4
docs.text: Converts this to a 4-dimensional vector, with the w-component equal to 1 (useful for homogeneous coordinates).

docs.title: toVector4Zero() : Vector4
docs.text: Converts this to a 4-dimensional vector, with the w-component equal to 0

docs.title: setXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : Vector3
docs.text: Sets all of the components of this vector, returning this.

docs.title: setX( x : number ) : Vector3
docs.text: Sets the x-component of this vector, returning this.

docs.title: setY( y : number ) : Vector3
docs.text: Sets the y-component of this vector, returning this.

docs.title: setZ( z : number ) : Vector3
docs.text: Sets the z-component of this vector, returning this.

docs.title: set( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Sets this vector to be a copy of another vector.

This is the mutable form of the function copy(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: setMagnitude( magnitude : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Sets the magnitude of this vector. If the passed-in magnitude is negative, this flips the vector and sets its magnitude to abs( magnitude ).

This is the mutable form of the function withMagnitude(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: add( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Adds another vector to this vector, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function plus(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: addXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Adds another vector (x,y,z) to this vector, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function plusXYZ(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: addScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Adds a scalar to this vector (added to every component), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function plusScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: subtract( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Subtracts this vector by another vector, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function minus(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: subtractXYZ( x : number, y : number, z : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Subtracts this vector by another vector (x,y,z), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function minusXYZ(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: subtractScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Subtracts this vector by a scalar (subtracts each component by the scalar), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function minusScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: multiplyScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Multiplies this vector by a scalar (multiplies each component by the scalar), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function timesScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: multiply( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Multiplies this vector by a scalar (multiplies each component by the scalar), changing this vector. Same as multiplyScalar.

This is the mutable form of the function times(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: componentMultiply( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Multiplies this vector by another vector component-wise, changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function componentTimes(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: divideScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector3
docs.text:
Divides this vector by a scalar (divides each component by the scalar), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function dividedScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: negate() : Vector3
docs.text:
Negates this vector (multiplies each component by -1), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function negated(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: setCross( v : Vector3 ) : Vector3
docs.text: Sets our value to the Euclidean 3-dimensional cross-product of this vector by the passed-in vector.

docs.title: normalize() : Vector3
docs.text:
Normalizes this vector (rescales to where the magnitude is 1), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function normalized(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: roundSymmetric() : Vector3
docs.text:
Rounds each component of this vector with Utils.roundSymmetric.

This is the mutable form of the function roundedSymmetric(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning the vector itself.

docs.text: The X coordinate of the vector.

docs.text: The Y coordinate of the vector.

docs.text: The Z coordinate of the vector.

Spherical linear interpolation between two unit vectors.

@param start - Start unit vector @param end - End unit vector @param ratio - Between 0 (at start vector) and 1 (at end vector) @returns Spherical linear interpolation between the start and end

This is the mutable form of the function componentTimes(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: divideScalar( scalar : number ) : Vector4
docs.text:
Divides this vector by a scalar (divides each component by the scalar), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function dividedScalar(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: negate() : Vector4
docs.text:
Negates this vector (multiplies each component by -1), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function negated(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: normalize() : Vector4
docs.text:
Normalizes this vector (rescales to where the magnitude is 1), changing this vector.

This is the mutable form of the function normalized(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning this vector itself.

docs.title: roundSymmetric() : Vector4
docs.text:
Rounds each component of this vector with Utils.roundSymmetric.

This is the mutable form of the function roundedSymmetric(). This will mutate (change) this vector, in addition to returning the vector itself.

docs.title: x : number
docs.text: The X coordinate of the vector.

docs.title: y : number
docs.text: The Y coordinate of the vector.

docs.title: z : number
docs.text: The Z coordinate of the vector.

docs.title: w : number
docs.text: The W coordinate of the vector.

docs.title: pool : Pool
docs.text: (readonly)

docs.title: Type Locale
docs.text:
Hard coding a few locales here is better than relying on a generated output of the "ground truth" localeData in babel, which could change at any time and cause a type error here (either on main or worse, in release branches). Also we could reach the TypeScript maximum for number of string union entries, (see https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/41160#issuecomment-1287271132). Feel free to add any locale here as needed for the type. JO 2024-12-23: This is blocking scenerystack manually specifying locales. JO: manually adding full list of locales.

Make the package.json contents available to the simulation, so it can access the version, sim name, etc.

@author Sam Reid (PhET Interactive Simulations)

docs.title: Source Code
docs.text: See the source for packageJSON.ts in the joist repository.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In this file:

regionAndCultureProperty is a global Property used to set aspects of i18n that are related to region and/or culture, but that do not pertain to language (see localeProperty for language).

The type RegionAndCulture defines the complete set of choices for regionAndCultureProperty. The choices supported by a sim are defined in package.json via "supportedRegionsAndCultures", and determines the value of supportedRegionAndCultureValues. Whether included explicitly or implicitly, 'usa' is always choice, because it is the fallback.

The initial value of regionAndCultureProperty can be specified in package.json and via a query parameter. In package.json, "defaultRegionAndCulture" identifies the initial choice, and defaults to 'usa'. Use the ?regionAndCulture query parameter to override the default, for example ?regionAndCulture=asia

@author Marla Schulz (PhET Interactive Simulations) @author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu> @author Chris Malley (PixelZoom, Inc.)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A circular arc (a continuous sub-part of a circle).

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.title: Class Arc
docs.text: import { Arc } from 'scenerystack/kite';

docs.title: new Arc( center : Vector2, radius : number, startAngle : number, endAngle : number, anticlockwise : boolean )
setCenter( center : Vector2 ) : this
docs.text: Sets the center of the Arc.

docs.title: getCenter() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the center of this Arc.

docs.title: setRadius( radius : number ) : this
docs.text: Sets the radius of the Arc.

docs.title: getRadius() : number
docs.text: Returns the radius of this Arc.

docs.title: setStartAngle( startAngle : number ) : this
docs.text: Sets the startAngle of the Arc.

docs.title: getStartAngle() : number
docs.text: Returns the startAngle of this Arc.

docs.title: setEndAngle( endAngle : number ) : this
docs.text: Sets the endAngle of the Arc.

docs.title: getEndAngle() : number
docs.text: Returns the endAngle of this Arc.

docs.title: setAnticlockwise( anticlockwise : boolean ) : this
docs.text: Sets the anticlockwise of the Arc.

docs.title: getAnticlockwise() : boolean
docs.text: Returns the anticlockwise of this Arc.

docs.title: positionAt( t : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Returns the position parametrically, with 0 <= t <= 1.

NOTE: positionAt( 0 ) will return the start of the segment, and positionAt( 1 ) will return the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: tangentAt( t : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Returns the non-normalized tangent (dx/dt, dy/dt) of this segment at the parametric value of t, with 0 <= t <= 1.

NOTE: tangentAt( 0 ) will return the tangent at the start of the segment, and tangentAt( 1 ) will return the tangent at the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: curvatureAt( t : number ) : number
docs.text:
Returns the signed curvature of the segment at the parametric value t, where 0 <= t <= 1.

The curvature will be positive for visual clockwise / mathematical counterclockwise curves, negative for opposite curvature, and 0 for no curvature.

NOTE: curvatureAt( 0 ) will return the curvature at the start of the segment, and curvatureAt( 1 ) will return the curvature at the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: subdivided( t : number ) : Arc[]
docs.text:
Returns an array with up to 2 sub-segments, split at the parametric t value. Together (in order) they should make up the same shape as the current segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: invalidate()
docs.text: Clears cached information, should be called when any of the 'constructor arguments' are mutated.

docs.title: getStart() : Vector2
docs.text: Gets the start position of this arc.

docs.title: getEnd() : Vector2
docs.text: Gets the end position of this arc.

docs.title: getStartTangent() : Vector2
docs.text: Gets the unit vector tangent to this arc at the start point.

docs.title: getEndTangent() : Vector2
docs.text: Gets the unit vector tangent to the arc at the end point.

docs.title: getActualEndAngle() : number
docs.text: Gets the end angle in radians.

docs.title: getIsFullPerimeter() : boolean
docs.text: Returns a boolean value that indicates if the arc wraps up by more than two Pi.

docs.title: getAngleDifference() : number
docs.text:
Returns an angle difference that represents how "much" of the circle our arc covers.

The answer is always greater or equal to zero The answer can exceed two Pi

docs.title: getBounds() : Bounds2
docs.text: Returns the bounds of this segment.

docs.title: getNondegenerateSegments() : Arc[]
docs.text: Returns a list of non-degenerate segments that are equivalent to this segment. Generally gets rid (or simplifies) invalid or repeated segments.

docs.title: mapAngle( angle : number ) : number
docs.text: Maps a contained angle to between [startAngle,actualEndAngle), even if the end angle is lower.

docs.title: tAtAngle( angle : number ) : number
docs.text: Returns the parametrized value t for a given angle. The value t should range from 0 to 1 (inclusive).

docs.title: angleAt( t : number ) : number
docs.text: Returns the angle for the parametrized t value. The t value should range from 0 to 1 (inclusive).

docs.title: positionAtAngle( angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the position of this arc at angle.

docs.title: tangentAtAngle( angle : number ) : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the normalized tangent of this arc. The tangent points outward (inward) of this arc for clockwise (anticlockwise) direction.

docs.title: containsAngle( angle : number ) : boolean
docs.text: Returns whether the given angle is contained by the arc (whether a ray from the arc's origin going in that angle will intersect the arc).

docs.title: getSVGPathFragment() : string
docs.text: Returns a string containing the SVG path. assumes that the start point is already provided, so anything that calls this needs to put the M calls first

docs.title: strokeLeft( lineWidth : number ) : Arc[]
docs.text: Returns an array of arcs that will draw an offset on the logical left side

docs.title: strokeRight( lineWidth : number ) : Arc[]
docs.text: Returns an array of arcs that will draw an offset curve on the logical right side

docs.title: getInteriorExtremaTs() : number[]
docs.text: Returns a list of t values where dx/dt or dy/dt is 0 where 0 < t < 1. subdividing on these will result in monotonic segments Does not include t=0 and t=1

docs.title: intersection( ray : Ray2 ) : RayIntersection[]
docs.text: Hit-tests this segment with the ray. An array of all intersections of the ray with this segment will be returned. For details, see the documentation in Segment.js

docs.title: windingIntersection( ray : Ray2 ) : number
docs.text: Returns the resultant winding number of this ray intersecting this arc.

docs.title: writeToContext( context : CanvasRenderingContext2D )
docs.text: Draws this arc to the 2D Canvas context, assuming the context's current location is already at the start point

docs.title: transformed( matrix : Matrix3 ) : Arc | EllipticalArc
docs.text:
Returns a new copy of this arc, transformed by the given matrix.

TODO: test various transform types, especially rotations, scaling, shears, etc. https://github.com/phetsims/kite/issues/76

docs.title: getSignedAreaFragment() : number
docs.text:
Returns the contribution to the signed area computed using Green's Theorem, with P=-y/2 and Q=x/2.

NOTE: This is this segment's contribution to the line integral (-y/2 dx + x/2 dy).

docs.title: reversed() : Arc
docs.text: Returns a reversed copy of this segment (mapping the parametrization from [0,1] => [1,0]).

docs.title: getArcLength() : number
docs.text: Returns the arc length of the segment.

docs.title: toPiecewiseLinearOrArcSegments() : Segment[]
docs.text: We can handle this simply by returning ourselves.

docs.title: serialize() : SerializedArc
docs.text: Returns an object form that can be turned back into a segment with the corresponding deserialize method.

docs.title: getOverlaps( segment : Segment, epsilon ) : Overlap[] | null
docs.text:
Determine whether two lines overlap over a continuous section, and if so finds the a,b pair such that p( t ) === q( a * t + b ).

@param segment @param [epsilon] - Will return overlaps only if no two corresponding points differ by this amount or more in one component. @returns - The solution, if there is one (and only one)

docs.title: getConicMatrix() : Matrix3
docs.text: Returns the matrix representation of the conic section of the circle. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matrix_representation_of_conic_sections

docs.title: deserialize( obj : SerializedArc ) : Arc
docs.text: Returns an Arc from the serialized representation.

docs.title: computeActualEndAngle( startAngle : number, endAngle : number, anticlockwise : boolean ) : number
docs.text:
Determines the actual end angle (compared to the start angle).

Normalizes the sign of the angles, so that the sign of ( endAngle - startAngle ) matches whether it is anticlockwise.

docs.title: getAngularOverlaps( startAngle1 : number, endAngle1 : number, startAngle2 : number, endAngle2 : number ) : Overlap[]
docs.text:
Determine whether two Arcs overlap over continuous sections, and if so finds the a,b pairs such that p( t ) === q( a * t + b ).

@param startAngle1 - Start angle of arc 1 @param endAngle1 - "Actual" end angle of arc 1 @param startAngle2 - Start angle of arc 2 @param endAngle2 - "Actual" end angle of arc 2 @returns - Any overlaps (from 0 to 2)

docs.title: getOverlaps( arc1 : Arc, arc2 : Arc ) : Overlap[]
docs.text:
Determine whether two Arcs overlap over continuous sections, and if so finds the a,b pairs such that p( t ) === q( a * t + b ).

@returns - Any overlaps (from 0 to 2)

docs.title: getCircleIntersectionPoint( center1 : Vector2, radius1 : number, center2 : Vector2, radius2 : number ) : Vector2[]
docs.text:
Returns the points of intersections between two circles.

@param center1 - Center of the first circle @param radius1 - Radius of the first circle @param center2 - Center of the second circle @param radius2 - Radius of the second circle

docs.title: intersect( a : Arc, b : Arc ) : SegmentIntersection[]
docs.text: Returns any (finite) intersection between the two arc segments.

docs.title: createFromPoints( startPoint : Vector2, middlePoint : Vector2, endPoint : Vector2 ) : Segment
docs.text: Creates an Arc (or if straight enough a Line) segment that goes from the startPoint to the endPoint, touching the middlePoint somewhere between the two.

docs.title: Type SerializedArc
docs.text:
import type { SerializedArc } from 'scenerystack/kite';
  • type: "Arc"
  • centerX: number
  • centerY: number
  • radius: number
  • startAngle: number
  • endAngle: number
  • anticlockwise: boolean

docs.title: Source Code
docs.text: See the source for Arc.ts in the kite repository.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A boundary is a loop of directed half-edges that always follow in the tightest counter-clockwise direction around vertices.

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.title: Class Boundary
docs.text: import { Boundary } from 'scenerystack/kite';

docs.title: initialize( halfEdges )
docs.text:
Similar to a usual constructor, but is set up so it can be called multiple times (with dispose() in-between) to support pooling. @private

@param {Array.<HalfEdge>} halfEdges @returns {Boundary} - This reference for chaining

docs.title: serialize()
docs.text:
Returns an object form that can be turned back into a segment with the corresponding deserialize method. @public

@returns {Object}

docs.title: dispose()
docs.text: Removes references (so it can allow other objects to be GC'ed or pooled), and frees itself to the pool so it can be reused. @public

docs.title: isInner()
docs.text:
Returns whether this boundary is essentially "counter-clockwise" (in the non-reversed coordinate system) with positive signed area, or "clockwise" with negative signed area. @public

Boundaries are treated as "inner" boundaries when they are counter-clockwise, as the path followed will generally follow the inside of a face (given how the "next" edge of a vertex is computed).

@returns {number}

docs.title: computeSignedArea()
docs.text:
Returns the signed area of this boundary, given its half edges. @public

Each half-edge has its own contribution to the signed area, which are summed together.

@returns {number}

docs.title: computeBounds()
docs.text:
Returns the bounds of the boundary (the union of each of the boundary's segments' bounds). @public

@returns {Bounds2}

docs.title: computeExtremePoint( transform )
docs.text:
Returns a point on the boundary which, when the shape (and point) are transformed with the given transform, would be a point with the minimal y value. @public

Will only return one point, even if there are multiple points that have the same minimal y values for the boundary. The point may be at the end of one of the edges/segments (at a vertex), but also may somewhere in the middle of an edge/segment.

@param {Transform3} transform - Transform used because we want the inverse also. @returns {Vector2}

docs.title: computeExtremeRay( transform )
docs.text:
Returns a ray (position and direction) pointing away from our boundary at an "extreme" point, so that the ray will be guaranteed not to intersect this boundary. @public

The ray's position will be slightly offset from the boundary, so that it will not technically intersect the boundary where the extreme point lies. The extreme point will be chosen such that it would have the smallest y value when the boundary is transformed by the given transformation.

The ray's direction will be such that if the ray is transformed by the given transform, it will be pointing in the negative-y direction (e.g. a vector of (0,-1)). This should guarantee it is facing away from the boundary, and will be consistent in direction with other extreme rays (needed for its use case with the boundary graph).

@param {Transform3} transform @returns {Ray2}

docs.title: hasHalfEdge( halfEdge )
docs.text:
Returns whether this boundary includes the specified half-edge. @public

@param {HalfEdge} halfEdge @returns {boolean}

docs.title: toSubpath()
docs.text:
Converts this boundary to a Subpath, so that we can construct things like Shape objects from it. @public

@returns {Subpath}

docs.title: freeToPool()
docs.text: @public

docs.title: pool : Pool
docs.text: @public

docs.title: Source Code
docs.text: See the source for Boundary.js in the kite repository.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A region of two segments that intersects (contains static functions for segment intersection).

BoundsIntersection.intersect( a, b ) should be used for most general intersection routines as a fallback. Other segment-specific routines may be much faster.

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.title: Class BoundsIntersection
docs.text: import { BoundsIntersection } from 'scenerystack/kite';

docs.title: new BoundsIntersection( a : Segment, b : Segment, atMin : number, atMax : number, btMin : number, btMax : number, aMin : Vector2, aMax : Vector2, bMin : Vector2, bMax : Vector2 )
initialize( a : Segment, b : Segment, atMin : number, atMax : number, btMin : number, btMax : number, aMin : Vector2, aMax : Vector2, bMin : Vector2, bMax : Vector2 ) : BoundsIntersection
docs.text: @param a @param b @param atMin - Lower t value for the region of the 'a' segment @param atMax - Higher t value for the region of the 'a' segment @param btMin - Lower t value for the region of the 'b' segment @param btMax - Higher t value for the region of the 'b' segment @param aMin - Location of the lower t value for the 'a' segment's region @param aMax - Location of the higher t value for the 'a' segment's region @param bMin - Location of the lower t value for the 'b' segment's region @param bMax - Location of the higher t value for the 'b' segment's region @returns - This reference for chaining

docs.title: distance( otherIntersection : BoundsIntersection ) : number
docs.text: A measure of distance between this and another intersection.

docs.title: clean()
docs.text: Removes references (so it can allow other objects to be GC'ed or pooled)

docs.title: a : Segment | null
docs.text: Null if cleaned of references

docs.title: aMin : Vector2 | null
docs.text: Null if cleaned of references

docs.title: intersect( a : Segment, b : Segment ) : SegmentIntersection[]
docs.text:
Determine (finite) points of intersection between two arbitrary segments.

Does repeated subdivision and excludes a-b region pairs that don't intersect. Doing this repeatedly narrows down intersections, to the point that they can be combined for a fairly accurate answer.

docs.text: (readonly)

setStart( start : Vector2 ) : this
docs.text: Sets the start point of the Cubic.

docs.title: getStart() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the start of this Cubic.

docs.title: setControl1( control1 : Vector2 ) : this
docs.text: Sets the first control point of the Cubic.

docs.title: getControl1() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the first control point of this Cubic.

docs.title: setControl2( control2 : Vector2 ) : this
docs.text: Sets the second control point of the Cubic.

docs.title: getControl2() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the second control point of this Cubic.

docs.title: setEnd( end : Vector2 ) : this
docs.text: Sets the end point of the Cubic.

docs.title: getEnd() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the end of this Cubic.

docs.title: positionAt( t : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Returns the position parametrically, with 0 <= t <= 1.

NOTE: positionAt( 0 ) will return the start of the segment, and positionAt( 1 ) will return the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: tangentAt( t : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Returns the non-normalized tangent (dx/dt, dy/dt) of this segment at the parametric value of t, with 0 <= t <= 1.

NOTE: tangentAt( 0 ) will return the tangent at the start of the segment, and tangentAt( 1 ) will return the tangent at the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: curvatureAt( t : number ) : number
docs.text:
Returns the signed curvature of the segment at the parametric value t, where 0 <= t <= 1.

The curvature will be positive for visual clockwise / mathematical counterclockwise curves, negative for opposite curvature, and 0 for no curvature.

NOTE: curvatureAt( 0 ) will return the curvature at the start of the segment, and curvatureAt( 1 ) will return the curvature at the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: subdivided( t : number ) : Cubic[]
docs.text:
Returns an array with up to 2 sub-segments, split at the parametric t value. Together (in order) they should make up the same shape as the current segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: invalidate()
docs.text: Clears cached information, should be called when any of the 'constructor arguments' are mutated.

docs.title: getStartTangent() : Vector2
docs.text: Gets the start position of this cubic polynomial.

docs.title: getEndTangent() : Vector2
docs.text: Gets the end position of this cubic polynomial.

docs.title: getR() : Vector2
docs.text: TODO: documentation https://github.com/phetsims/kite/issues/76

docs.title: getS() : Vector2
docs.text: TODO: documentation https://github.com/phetsims/kite/issues/76

docs.title: getTCusp() : number
docs.text: Returns the parametric t value for the possible cusp location. A cusp may or may not exist at that point.

docs.title: getTDeterminant() : number
docs.text: Returns the determinant value for the cusp, which indicates the presence (or lack of presence) of a cusp.

docs.title: getTInflection1() : number
docs.text: Returns the parametric t value for the potential location of the first possible inflection point.

docs.title: getTInflection2() : number
docs.text: Returns the parametric t value for the potential location of the second possible inflection point.

docs.title: getQuadratics() : Quadratic[] | null
docs.text: If there is a cusp, this cubic will consist of one or two quadratic segments, typically "start => cusp" and "cusp => end".

docs.title: getXExtremaT() : number[]
docs.text: Returns a list of parametric t values where x-extrema exist, i.e. where dx/dt==0. These are candidate locations on the cubic for "maximum X" and "minimum X", and are needed for bounds computations.

docs.title: getYExtremaT() : number[]
docs.text: Returns a list of parametric t values where y-extrema exist, i.e. where dy/dt==0. These are candidate locations on the cubic for "maximum Y" and "minimum Y", and are needed for bounds computations.

docs.title: getBounds() : Bounds2
docs.text: Returns the bounds of this segment.

docs.title: getNondegenerateSegments() : Segment[]
docs.text: Returns a list of non-degenerate segments that are equivalent to this segment. Generally gets rid (or simplifies) invalid or repeated segments.

docs.title: hasCusp() : boolean
docs.text: Returns whether this cubic has a cusp.

docs.title: offsetTo( r : number, reverse : boolean ) : Line[]
getSVGPathFragment() : string
docs.text: Returns a string containing the SVG path. assumes that the start point is already provided, so anything that calls this needs to put the M calls first

docs.text: Returns an array of lines that will draw an offset curve on the logical left side

docs.title: strokeRight( lineWidth : number ) : Line[]
docs.text: Returns an array of lines that will draw an offset curve on the logical right side

docs.title: getInteriorExtremaTs() : number[]
docs.text: Returns a list of t values where dx/dt or dy/dt is 0 where 0 < t < 1. subdividing on these will result in monotonic segments The list does not include t=0 and t=1

docs.title: windingIntersection( ray : Ray2 ) : number
docs.text: Returns the winding number for intersection with a ray

docs.text: Returns a degree-reduced quadratic Bezier if possible, otherwise it returns null

Returns the contribution to the signed area computed using Green's Theorem, with P=-y/2 and Q=x/2.

NOTE: This is this segment's contribution to the line integral (-y/2 dx + x/2 dy).

@returns - Null if there is no intersection

docs.title: getOverlaps( segment : Segment, epsilon ) : Overlap[] | null
docs.text:
Determine whether two lines overlap over a continuous section, and if so finds the a,b pair such that p( t ) === q( a * t + b ).

@param segment @param [epsilon] - Will return overlaps only if no two corresponding points differ by this amount or more in one component. @returns - The solution, if there is one (and only one)

docs.title: degree : number
docs.text: Degree of this polynomial (cubic)

docs.title: deserialize( obj : SerializedCubic ) : Cubic
docs.text: Returns a Cubic from the serialized representation.

docs.title: extremaT( v0 : number, v1 : number, v2 : number, v3 : number ) : number[]
docs.text: Finds what t values the cubic extrema are at (if any). This is just the 1-dimensional case, used for multiple purposes

docs.title: getOverlaps( cubic1 : Cubic, cubic2 : Cubic, epsilon ) : Overlap[]
docs.text:
Determine whether two Cubics overlap over a continuous section, and if so finds the a,b pair such that p( t ) === q( a * t + b ).

NOTE: for this particular function, we assume we're not degenerate. Things may work if we can be degree-reduced to a quadratic, but generally that shouldn't be done.

@param cubic1 @param cubic2 @param [epsilon] - Will return overlaps only if no two corresponding points differ by this amount or more in one component. @returns - The solution, if there is one (and only one)

docs.title: Overview

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A face is usually contained by an ("inner") boundary of edges, and zero or more ("outer") boundary holes on the inside. The naming is somewhat counterintuitive here, because the "inner" boundaries are on the inside of the edges (towards our face), and the "outer" hole boundaries are on the outer half-edges of the holes.

There is normally one "unbounded" face without a normal boundary, whose "area" expands to infinity, and contains the everything on the exterior of all of the edges.

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.text: Removes references (so it can allow other objects to be GC'ed or pooled), and frees itself to the pool so it can be reused. @public

docs.title: addBoundaryFaceReferences( boundary )
docs.text:
Marks all half-edges on the boundary as belonging to this face. @public

@param {Boundary} boundary

docs.title: recursivelyAddHoles( outerBoundary )
docs.text:
Processes the boundary-graph for a given outer boundary, and turns it into holes for this face. @public

In the graph, every outer boundary in each connected component will be holes for the single inner boundary (which will be, in this case, our face's boundary). Since it's a tree, we can walk the tree recursively to add all necessary holes.

@param {Boundary} outerBoundary

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A multigraph whose edges are segments.

Supports general shape simplification, overlap/intersection removal and computation. General output would include Shapes (from CAG - Constructive Area Geometry) and triangulations.

See Graph.binaryResult for the general procedure for CAG.

TODO: Use https://github.com/mauriciosantos/Buckets-JS for priority queue, implement simple sweep line https://github.com/phetsims/kite/issues/76 with "enters" and "leaves" entries in the queue. When edge removed, remove "leave" from queue. and add any replacement edges. Applies to overlap and intersection handling. NOTE: This should impact performance a lot, as we are currently over-scanning and re-scanning a lot. Intersection is currently (by far?) the performance bottleneck. TODO: Collapse non-Line adjacent edges together. Similar logic to overlap for each segment time, hopefully can factor this out. TODO: Properly handle sorting edges around a vertex when two edges have the same tangent out. We'll need to use curvature, or do tricks to follow both curves by an 'epsilon' and sort based on that. TODO: Consider separating out epsilon values (may be a general Kite thing rather than just ops) TODO: Loop-Blinn output and constrained Delaunay triangulation

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

@param {number} shapeId - The ID which should be shared for all paths/shapes that should be combined with respect to the winding number of faces. For CAG, independent shapes should be given different IDs (so they have separate winding numbers recorded). @param {Shape} shape @param {Object} [options] - See addSubpath

Adds a subpath of a Shape (with a given ID for CAG purposes) to the graph. @public

@param {number} shapeId - See addShape() documentation @param {Subpath} subpath @param {Object} [options]

docs.title: computeSimplifiedFaces()
docs.text: Simplifies edges/vertices, computes boundaries and faces (with the winding map). @public

docs.title: computeFaceInclusion( windingMapFilter )
docs.text:
Sets whether each face should be filled or unfilled based on a filter function. @public

The windingMapFilter will be called on each face's winding map, and will use the return value as whether the face is filled or not.

The winding map is an {Object} associated with each face that has a key for every shapeId that was used in addShape/addSubpath, and the value for those keys is the winding number of the face given all paths with the shapeId.

For example, imagine you added two shapeIds (0 and 1), and the iteration is on a face that is included in one loop specified with shapeId:0 (inside a counter-clockwise curve), and is outside of any segments specified by the second loop (shapeId:1). Then the winding map will be: { 0: 1 // shapeId:0 has a winding number of 1 for this face (generally filled) 1: 0 // shapeId:1 has a winding number of 0 for this face (generally not filled) }

Generally, winding map filters can be broken down into two steps: 1. Given the winding number for each shapeId, compute whether that loop was originally filled. Normally, this is done with a non-zero rule (any winding number is filled, except zero). SVG also provides an even-odd rule (odd numbers are filled, even numbers are unfilled). 2. Given booleans for each shapeId from step 1, compute CAG operations based on boolean formulas. Say you wanted to take the union of shapeIds 0 and 1, then remove anything in shapeId 2. Given the booleans above, this can be directly computed as (filled0 || filled1) && !filled2.

@param {function} windingMapFilter

docs.title: createFilledSubGraph()
docs.text:
Create a new Graph object based only on edges in this graph that separate a "filled" face from an "unfilled" face. @public

This is a convenient way to "collapse" adjacent filled and unfilled faces together, and compute the curves and holes properly, given a filled "normal" graph.

docs.title: facesToShape()
docs.text:
Returns a Shape that creates a subpath for each filled face (with the desired holes). @public

Generally should be called on a graph created with createFilledSubGraph().

@returns {Shape}

docs.title: dispose()
docs.text: Releases owned objects to their pools, and clears references that may have been picked up from external sources. @public

docs.title: addEdge( edge )
docs.text:
Adds an edge to the graph (and sets up connection information). @private

@param {Edge} edge

docs.title: removeEdge( edge )
docs.text:
Removes an edge from the graph (and disconnects incident information). @private

@param {Edge} edge

docs.title: replaceEdgeInLoops( edge, forwardHalfEdges )
docs.text:
Replaces a single edge (in loops) with a series of edges (possibly empty). @private

@param {Edge} edge @param {Array.<HalfEdge>} forwardHalfEdges

docs.title: collapseAdjacentEdges()
docs.text:
Tries to combine adjacent edges (with a 2-order vertex) into one edge where possible. @private

This helps to combine things like collinear lines, where there's a vertex that can basically be removed.

docs.title: eliminateOverlap()
docs.text: Gets rid of overlapping segments by combining overlaps into a shared edge. @private

docs.title: splitOverlap( aEdge, bEdge, overlap )
docs.text:
Splits/combines edges when there is an overlap of two edges (two edges who have an infinite number of intersection points). @private

NOTE: This does NOT dispose aEdge/bEdge, due to eliminateOverlap's needs.

Generally this creates an edge for the "shared" part of both segments, and then creates edges for the parts outside of the shared region, connecting them together.

@param {Edge} aEdge @param {Edge} bEdge @param {Overlap} overlap @returns {Array.<Edge>}

docs.title: eliminateSelfIntersection()
docs.text: Handles splitting of self-intersection of segments (happens with Cubics). @private

docs.title: eliminateIntersection()
docs.text: Replace intersections between different segments by splitting them and creating a vertex. @private

docs.title: simpleSplit( aEdge, bEdge, aT, bT, point )
docs.text:
Handles splitting two intersecting edges. @private

@param {Edge} aEdge @param {Edge} bEdge @param {number} aT - Parametric t value of the intersection for aEdge @param {number} bT - Parametric t value of the intersection for bEdge @param {Vector2} point - Location of the intersection

@returns {{addedEdges: Edge[], removedEdges: Edge[]}|null}

docs.title: splitEdge( edge, t, vertex )
docs.text:
Splits an edge into two edges at a specific parametric t value. @private

@param {Edge} edge @param {number} t @param {Vertex} vertex - The vertex that is placed at the split location

docs.title: collapseVertices()
docs.text: Combine vertices that are almost exactly in the same place (removing edges and vertices where necessary). @private

docs.title: markBridges( bridges, vertex )
docs.text:
Scan a given vertex for bridges recursively with a depth-first search. @private

Records visit times to each vertex, and back-propagates so that we can efficiently determine if there was another path around to the vertex.

Assumes this is only called one time once all edges/vertices are set up. Repeated calls will fail because we don't mark visited/etc. references again on startup

See Tarjan's algorithm for more information. Some modifications were needed, since this is technically a multigraph/pseudograph (can have edges that have the same start/end vertex, and can have multiple edges going from the same two vertices).

@param {Array.<Edge>} bridges - Appends bridge edges to here. @param {Vertex} vertex

docs.title: removeBridges()
docs.text: Removes edges that are the only edge holding two connected components together. Based on our problem, the face on either side of the "bridge" edges would always be the same, so we can safely remove them. @private

docs.title: removeLowOrderVertices()
docs.text: Removes vertices that have order less than 2 (so either a vertex with one or zero edges adjacent). @private

docs.title: orderVertexEdges()
docs.text: Sorts incident half-edges for each vertex. @private

docs.title: extractFaces()
docs.text: Creates boundaries and faces by following each half-edge counter-clockwise @private

docs.title: computeBoundaryTree()
docs.text:
Given the inner and outer boundaries, it computes a tree representation to determine what boundaries are holes of what other boundaries, then sets up face holes with the result. @public

This information is stored in the childBoundaries array of Boundary, and is then read out to set up faces.

docs.title: computeWindingMap()
docs.text: Computes the winding map for each face, starting with 0 on the unbounded face (for each shapeId). @private

docs.title: computeDifferential( edge, shapeId )
docs.text:
Computes the differential in winding numbers (forward face winding number minus the reversed face winding number) ("forward face" is the face on the forward half-edge side, etc.) @private

@param {Edge} edge @param {number} shapeId @returns {number} - The difference between forward face and reversed face winding numbers.

docs.title: fillAlternatingFaces()
docs.text:
Sets the unbounded face as unfilled, and then sets each face's fill so that edges separate one filled face with one unfilled face. @private

NOTE: Best to call this on the result from createFilledSubGraph(), since it should have guaranteed properties to make this consistent. Notably, all vertices need to have an even order (number of edges)

docs.title: getBoundaryOfHalfEdge( halfEdge )
docs.text:
Returns the boundary that contains the specified half-edge. @private

TODO: find a better way, this is crazy inefficient https://github.com/phetsims/kite/issues/76

@param {HalfEdge} halfEdge @returns {Boundary}

docs.title: BINARY_NONZERO_UNION( windingMap )
docs.text:
"Union" binary winding map filter for use with Graph.binaryResult. @public

This combines both shapes together so that a point is in the resulting shape if it was in either of the input shapes.

@param {Object} windingMap - See computeFaceInclusion for more details @returns {boolean}

docs.title: BINARY_NONZERO_INTERSECTION( windingMap )
docs.text:
"Intersection" binary winding map filter for use with Graph.binaryResult. @public

This combines both shapes together so that a point is in the resulting shape if it was in both of the input shapes.

@param {Object} windingMap - See computeFaceInclusion for more details @returns {boolean}

docs.title: BINARY_NONZERO_DIFFERENCE( windingMap )
docs.text:
"Difference" binary winding map filter for use with Graph.binaryResult. @public

This combines both shapes together so that a point is in the resulting shape if it was in the first shape AND was NOT in the second shape.

@param {Object} windingMap - See computeFaceInclusion for more details @returns {boolean}

docs.title: BINARY_NONZERO_XOR( windingMap )
docs.text:
"XOR" binary winding map filter for use with Graph.binaryResult. @public

This combines both shapes together so that a point is in the resulting shape if it is only in exactly one of the input shapes. It's like the union minus intersection.

@param {Object} windingMap - See computeFaceInclusion for more details @returns {boolean}

docs.title: binaryResult( shapeA, shapeB, windingMapFilter )
docs.text:
Returns the resulting Shape obtained by combining the two shapes given with the filter. @public

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Represents a single direction/side of an Edge. There are two half-edges for each edge, representing each direction. The half-edge also stores face information for the face that would be to the left of the direction of travel.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A line segment (all points directly between the start and end point)

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.title: Class KiteLine
docs.text: import { KiteLine } from 'scenerystack/kite';

docs.title: new KiteLine( start : Vector2, end : Vector2 )
setStart( start : Vector2 ) : this
docs.text: Sets the start point of the Line.

docs.title: getStart() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the start of this Line.

docs.title: setEnd( end : Vector2 ) : this
docs.text: Sets the end point of the Line.

docs.title: getEnd() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the end of this Line.

docs.title: positionAt( t : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Returns the position parametrically, with 0 <= t <= 1.

NOTE: positionAt( 0 ) will return the start of the segment, and positionAt( 1 ) will return the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: tangentAt( t : number ) : Vector2
docs.text:
Returns the non-normalized tangent (dx/dt, dy/dt) of this segment at the parametric value of t, with 0 <= t <= 1.

NOTE: tangentAt( 0 ) will return the tangent at the start of the segment, and tangentAt( 1 ) will return the tangent at the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: curvatureAt( t : number ) : number
docs.text:
Returns the signed curvature of the segment at the parametric value t, where 0 <= t <= 1.

The curvature will be positive for visual clockwise / mathematical counterclockwise curves, negative for opposite curvature, and 0 for no curvature.

NOTE: curvatureAt( 0 ) will return the curvature at the start of the segment, and curvatureAt( 1 ) will return the curvature at the end of the segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: subdivided( t : number ) : Segment[]
docs.text:
Returns an array with up to 2 sub-segments, split at the parametric t value. Together (in order) they should make up the same shape as the current segment.

This method is part of the Segment API. See Segment.js's constructor for more API documentation.

docs.title: invalidate()
docs.text: Clears cached information, should be called when any of the 'constructor arguments' are mutated.

docs.title: getStartTangent() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns a normalized unit vector that is tangent to this line (at the starting point) the unit vectors points toward the end points.

docs.title: getEndTangent() : Vector2
docs.text: Returns the normalized unit vector that is tangent to this line same as getStartTangent, since this is a straight line

docs.title: getBounds() : Bounds2
docs.text: Returns the bounds of this segment.

docs.title: getBoundsWithTransform( matrix : Matrix3 ) : Bounds2
docs.text: Returns the bounding box for this transformed Line

docs.title: getNondegenerateSegments() : Segment[]
docs.text: Returns a list of non-degenerate segments that are equivalent to this segment. Generally gets rid (or simplifies) invalid or repeated segments.

docs.title: getSVGPathFragment() : string
docs.text: Returns a string containing the SVG path. assumes that the start point is already provided, so anything that calls this needs to put the M calls first

docs.title: strokeLeft( lineWidth : number ) : Line[]
docs.text: Returns an array of Line that will draw an offset curve on the logical left side

docs.title: strokeRight( lineWidth : number ) : Line[]
docs.text: Returns an array of Line that will draw an offset curve on the logical right side

docs.title: getInteriorExtremaTs() : number[]
docs.text: In general, this method returns a list of t values where dx/dt or dy/dt is 0 where 0 < t < 1. subdividing on these will result in monotonic segments Since lines are already monotone, it returns an empty array.

docs.title: intersection( ray : Ray2 ) : RayIntersection[]
docs.text: Hit-tests this segment with the ray. An array of all intersections of the ray with this segment will be returned. For details, see the documentation in Segment.js

docs.title: windingIntersection( ray : Ray2 ) : number
docs.text: Returns the resultant winding number of a ray intersecting this line.

docs.title: writeToContext( context : CanvasRenderingContext2D )
docs.text: Draws this line to the 2D Canvas context, assuming the context's current location is already at the start point

docs.title: transformed( matrix : Matrix3 ) : Line
docs.text: Returns a new Line that represents this line after transformation by the matrix

docs.title: explicitClosestToPoint( point : Vector2 ) : ClosestToPointResult[]
docs.text: Returns an object that gives information about the closest point (on a line segment) to the point argument

docs.title: getSignedAreaFragment() : number
docs.text:
Returns the contribution to the signed area computed using Green's Theorem, with P=-y/2 and Q=x/2.

NOTE: This is this segment's contribution to the line integral (-y/2 dx + x/2 dy).

docs.title: reparameterized( a : number, b : number ) : Line
docs.text: Given the current curve parameterized by t, will return a curve parameterized by x where t = a * x + b

docs.title: reversed() : Line
docs.text: Returns a reversed copy of this segment (mapping the parametrization from [0,1] => [1,0]).

docs.title: polarToCartesian( options : PiecewiseLinearOptions ) : Segment[]
docs.text:
Convert a line in the \((theta,r)\) plane of the form \((\theta_1,r_1)\) to \((\theta_2,r_2)\) and converts to the cartesian coordinate system

E.g. a polar line (0,1) to (2 Pi,1) would be mapped to a circle of radius 1

docs.title: getArcLength() : number
docs.text: Returns the arc length of the segment.

docs.title: toPiecewiseLinearOrArcSegments() : Segment[]
docs.text: We can handle this simply by returning ourselves.

docs.text: Returns an object form that can be turned back into a segment with the corresponding deserialize method.

docs.title: getOverlaps( segment : Segment, epsilon ) : Overlap[] | null
docs.text:
Determine whether two lines overlap over a continuous section, and if so finds the a,b pair such that p( t ) === q( a * t + b ).

@param segment @param [epsilon] - Will return overlaps only if no two corresponding points differ by this amount or more in one component. @returns - The solution, if there is one (and only one)

docs.text: Returns a Line from the serialized representation.

docs.text:
Determine whether two lines overlap over a continuous section, and if so finds the a,b pair such that p( t ) === q( a * t + b ).

@param line1 @param line2 @param [epsilon] - Will return overlaps only if no two corresponding points differ by this amount or more in one component. @returns - The solution, if there is one (and only one)

docs.text: Returns any (finite) intersection between the two line segments.

This should be more optimized than the general intersection routine of arbitrary segments.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Styles needed to determine a stroked line shape. Generally immutable.

Mirrors much of what is done with SVG/Canvas, see https://svgwg.org/svg2-draft/painting.html for details.

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>

docs.title: Class LineStyles
docs.text: import { LineStyles } from 'scenerystack/kite';

docs.title: equals( other : LineStyles ) : boolean
docs.text: Determines of this lineStyles is equal to the other LineStyles

docs.title: copy() : LineStyles
docs.text: Returns a copy of this LineStyles.

docs.title: leftJoin( center : Vector2, fromTangent : Vector2, toTangent : Vector2 ) : Segment[]
docs.text:
Creates an array of Segments that make up a line join, to the left side.

Joins two segments together on the logical "left" side, at 'center' (where they meet), and un-normalized tangent vectors in the direction of the stroking. To join on the "right" side, switch the tangent order and negate them.

docs.title: rightJoin( center : Vector2, fromTangent : Vector2, toTangent : Vector2 ) : Segment[]
docs.text:
Creates an array of Segments that make up a line join, to the right side.

Joins two segments together on the logical "right" side, at 'center' (where they meet), and normalized tangent vectors in the direction of the stroking. To join on the "left" side, switch the tangent order and negate them.

docs.title: cap( center : Vector2, tangent : Vector2 ) : Segment[]
docs.text: Creates an array of Segments that make up a line cap from the endpoint 'center' in the direction of the tangent

docs.title: lineWidth : number
docs.text: The width of the line (will be offset to each side by lineWidth/2)

docs.title: lineCap : LineCap
docs.text: 'butt', 'round' or 'square' - Controls appearance at endpoints for non-closed subpaths. - butt: straight-line at end point, going through the endpoint (perpendicular to the tangent) - round: circular border with radius lineWidth/2 around endpoints - square: straight-line past the end point (by lineWidth/2) See: https://svgwg.org/svg2-draft/painting.html#LineCaps

docs.title: lineJoin : LineJoin
docs.text: 'miter', 'round' or 'bevel' - Controls appearance at joints between segments (at the point) - miter: Use sharp corners (which aren't too sharp, see miterLimit). Extends edges until they meed. - round: circular border with radius lineWidth/2 around joints - bevel: directly joins the gap with a line segment. See: https://svgwg.org/svg2-draft/painting.html#LineJoin

docs.title: lineDash : number[]
docs.text: Even values in the array are the "dash" length, odd values are the "gap" length. NOTE: If there is an odd number of entries, it behaves like lineDash.concat( lineDash ). See: https://svgwg.org/svg2-draft/painting.html#StrokeDashing

docs.title: lineDashOffset : number
docs.text: Offset from the start of the subpath where the start of the line-dash array starts.

docs.title: miterLimit : number
docs.text: When to cut off lineJoin:miter to look like lineJoin:bevel. See https://svgwg.org/svg2-draft/painting.html

"butt" | "round" | "square"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A directed set of half-edges determined by how the original shapes/subpaths were directionally. This is distinct from boundaries, as: 1. Input shapes/subpaths can self-intersect, ignore clockwise restrictions, and avoid boundary restrictions. 2. Input shapes/subpaths can repeat over the same edges multiple times (affecting winding order), and can even double-back or do other operations. 3. We need to record separate shape IDs for the different loops, so we can perform CAG operations on separate ones. This means we need to track winding order separately for each ID.

As operations simplify/remove/replace edges, it will handle replacement of the edges in the loops.

@author Jonathan Olson <jonathan.olson@colorado.edu>
